title: Active Reading in MLA
description: Assessment of the concept that active reading is a deliberate process, not passive scanning.

questions.prompt: Which action best shows active reading?

choices.text: Skimming the text once to finish quickly
choices.feedback: This is passive reading because it focuses on getting through the text rather than tracking meaning.

choices.text: Annotating key ideas, asking questions, and noticing your thinking as you read
choices.feedback: This is correct because active reading requires deliberate actions such as annotation, questioning, and monitoring your thinking.

choices.text: Waiting until the end to decide whether the text made sense
choices.feedback: This misses the concept because active reading happens during reading, not only after the text is finished.

choices.text: Copying every sentence from the text into your notebook
choices.feedback: Copying everything does not show purposeful reading. Active reading focuses on important ideas and thinking moves.

questions.prompt: A student writes in the margin, "Why did the author compare the two schools here?" What active-reading habit is the student using most directly?

choices.feedback: This is correct because the student is asking a focused question to better understand the author's meaning.
This is incorrect because the student is engaging with confusion, not ignoring it.
Memorizing wording is not the main strategy shown here. The student is questioning to deepen understanding.

choices.text: Summarizing after finishing the whole text
choices.feedback: Summarizing is a different skill. This example shows questioning while reading.

questions.prompt: While reading an article about school lunches, a student underlines the sentence "Many students skip breakfast before arriving" and writes, "This might explain why the program matters." Why is this an effective active-reading move?

choices.text: It connects an important detail to a developing idea in the text
choices.feedback: This is correct because active reading includes marking evidence and tracking how details contribute to meaning.

choices.text: It replaces the need to read the rest of the article
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because one annotation does not replace full reading or continued thinking.

choices.text: It proves the student already knows the author's final conclusion
choices.feedback: The note suggests an inference, not total certainty about the entire text.

choices.text: It changes the author's words into the student's opinion only
choices.feedback: The annotation is tied to evidence from the text, so it is not just unsupported opinion.

questions.prompt: Which reader response best demonstrates tracking thinking during reading?

choices.text: I finished the article, so I must understand it.
choices.feedback: Finishing a text does not automatically show active thinking or comprehension.

choices.text: I highlighted three words because my teacher told me to.
choices.feedback: This may be a task completion move, but it does not clearly show reflection on meaning.

choices.text: At first I thought the article was praising the change, but after paragraph three I noticed a concern about fairness.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the reader is noticing how understanding changes while reading.

choices.text: I copied the title and the author's name into my notes.
choices.feedback: Recording basic information does not show the reader tracking their understanding of the text.

questions.prompt: True or False: Active reading means a reader should interact with the text while reading instead of only looking at the words.

choices.feedback: This is correct because active reading is a deliberate process of annotating, questioning, and monitoring meaning during reading.
This is incorrect because active reading does require interaction with the text while you read.
